nativeRange.habitat: Deepwater reef slopes, typically found at 35-65 m depth, most common around 50-60 m on patch reefs over sand or rubble.

decorPreferences: live rock with caves and overhangs

diet.notes: Wild diet consists primarily of zooplankton picked from the water column. In aquaria, generally accepts frozen mysis, enriched brine shrimp, and finely-chopped marine meats. Multiple small feedings per day (2-4) tend to maintain body condition better than once-daily feeds.

summary: The Sunset Anthias is a small, deep-reef fish from the Indo-West Pacific, prized for its warm orange coloration. It is generally considered peaceful and reef-safe, and does well in well-established systems with calm tankmates and multiple small feedings each day.
careNotes: The Sunset Anthias is a small, deep-water anthias native to the Indo-West Pacific, where it is found on reef slopes and patch reefs at depths of 35 to 65 metres — considerably deeper than many anthias commonly kept in aquariums. The species is also known by the scientific name Pyronotanthias parvirostris following a reclassification, though the older name Pseudanthias parvirostris remains the most widely used in the aquarium trade. Compared with shallow-water anthias, this species tends to be more reserved and is generally considered a good fit for peaceful reef systems with established food chains. It is best placed in dimmer or shaded areas of the aquarium to reflect its natural deep-reef habitat; very bright lighting may cause it to retreat and hide. A tightly-sealed lid is strongly advised, as anthias are widely regarded as accomplished jumpers. Anthias have high metabolisms and tend to lose condition quickly if underfed; multiple small feedings throughout the day — typically two to four — are generally recommended over a single daily meal. Like other members of its group, the Sunset Anthias is a protogynous sequential hermaphrodite, meaning individuals are born female and the dominant fish in a group will transition to male over time. In the wild, this species forms harems of one male and several females; hobbyists who want to keep a mixed group are generally advised to start with several juveniles or females and allow the hierarchy to develop naturally.
